title: Executive Function (in depth) - Fill in the Blank

questions.question: Franz Gall proposed ___, the (incorrect) idea that mental functions are localised and skull shape reflects them.
questions.blank_sentence: Franz Gall proposed ___, the (incorrect) idea that mental functions are localised and skull shape reflects them.
questions.explanation: Gall's phrenology was wrong but historically important — it encouraged research into brain localisation, paving the way for later experimental neuroscience like Hitzig & Fritsch's stimulation studies.

questions.question: Hitzig & Fritsch found that stimulating the ___ frontal lobe in dogs caused contralateral movement, while stimulating anterior frontal cortex produced no motor or sensory effect.
questions.blank_sentence: Hitzig & Fritsch found that stimulating the ___ frontal lobe in dogs caused contralateral movement, while stimulating anterior frontal cortex produced no motor or sensory effect.
questions.explanation: Hitzig & Fritsch (late 1860s) showed posterior frontal stimulation drives contralateral movement (motor cortex). The 'silent' anterior frontal cortex was therefore speculated to support higher cognitive functions — an early link between PFC and executive function.

questions.question: The ___ syndrome (lateral PFC damage) features apathy, failure to plan or initiate, lack of insight, and can produce abulia.
questions.blank_sentence: The ___ syndrome (lateral PFC damage) features apathy, failure to plan or initiate, lack of insight, and can produce abulia.
questions.explanation: The frontal dysexecutive syndrome arises from lateral PFC damage. Patients show apathy, planning failure, attention deficits, and abulia (lethargy, slow, distractible) — contrasting with the disinhibition syndrome from ventral/medial PFC.

questions.question: Antonio Damasio's term ___ sociopathy describes vmPFC-damage patients who can verbalise moral rules but act impulsively, with blunted affect and emotional outbursts.
questions.blank_sentence: Antonio Damasio's term ___ sociopathy describes vmPFC-damage patients who can verbalise moral rules but act impulsively, with blunted affect and emotional outbursts.
questions.explanation: Acquired sociopathy follows adult-onset vmPFC damage: patients know the rules but cannot act on them. Early-life vmPFC damage instead produces congenital sociopathy, where patients cannot even articulate the moral rules.

questions.question: On the Wisconsin Card Sorting Test, PFC patients show ___ — continuing to apply a previously valid rule despite repeated negative feedback.
questions.blank_sentence: On the Wisconsin Card Sorting Test, PFC patients show ___ — continuing to apply a previously valid rule despite repeated negative feedback.
questions.explanation: Milner (1963) first reported perseveration as the hallmark of PFC dysfunction on the WCST. Crucially, this is NOT a learning deficit — patients learn new rules normally; the problem is failure to disengage from the old rule.

questions.question: Damage to the ___ cortex (FPC) selectively impairs performance on complex Raven's Progressive Matrices and other tasks requiring relational integration.
questions.blank_sentence: Damage to the ___ cortex (FPC) selectively impairs performance on complex Raven's Progressive Matrices and other tasks requiring relational integration.
questions.explanation: Frontopolar cortex (FPC / aPFC) is the most anterior PFC region and supports relating multiple rules simultaneously. Smith, Keramatian & Christoff (2007) showed FPC activation specific to relational integration trials.

questions.question: Kerns et al. (2004) showed that high ACC activation on trial N predicts higher ___ activation on trial N+1, supporting a conflict-then-control hand-off.
questions.blank_sentence: Kerns et al. (2004) showed that high ACC activation on trial N predicts higher ___ activation on trial N+1, supporting a conflict-then-control hand-off.
questions.explanation: The Kerns et al. (2004) finding is that ACC signals conflict but does not resolve it — instead it cues lateral PFC (dlPFC) to adjust behaviour on the next trial. This was a foundational result for the original conflict-monitoring model.

questions.question: Behrens et al. (2007) recast dmPFC activity as tracking environmental ___ — how unstable reward contingencies are over time — rather than purely conflict.
questions.blank_sentence: Behrens et al. (2007) recast dmPFC activity as tracking environmental ___ — how unstable reward contingencies are over time — rather than purely conflict.
questions.explanation: Behrens et al. (2007) reframed dmPFC as encoding the mismatch between expectations and outcomes — a learning signal that tracks environmental volatility. This account fits both the lesion data (Fellows & Farah) and the reward-related activations that broke the original conflict model.

questions.question: In the Funahashi, Bruce & Goldman-Rakic (1989) oculomotor delayed response task, dlPFC neurons showed ___-period activity, a sustained discharge across the 3-second delay that was content-specific.
questions.blank_sentence: In the Funahashi, Bruce & Goldman-Rakic (1989) oculomotor delayed response task, dlPFC neurons showed ___-period activity, a sustained discharge across the 3-second delay that was content-specific.
questions.explanation: Delay-period activity is the sustained firing of dlPFC neurons across the memory delay, with each neuron tuned to a preferred location (memory field). When delay-period firing broke down, monkeys made errors — establishing the canonical neural signature of working memory.

questions.question: The ___ model of working memory (favoured at the neural level over Baddeley) holds that activated representations decay unless rehearsed, and only ~4 items fall within the focus of executive control.
questions.blank_sentence: The ___ model of working memory (favoured at the neural level over Baddeley) holds that activated representations decay unless rehearsed, and only ~4 items fall within the focus of executive control.
questions.explanation: Cowan's (1998) model proposes a single long-term store with activated representations that decay without rehearsal, plus a ~4-item focus of executive control. It is better supported by current neuroscience than Baddeley's multi-store model because it predicts WM content stays in sensory cortex — confirmed by Harrison & Tong (2009).
